subject: Re: Windows machine configurations
body:
Dear Dmitry,

Do you have any concerns with using a Ryzen CPU?  For example, does M use
SIMD instructions?  How about the Intel MKL?

What's your guidance on the number of cores required?

The Zen 3 chips will be released November 5, and they seem pretty great.

Matt


On Mon, Oct 12, 2020 at 1:24 PM teg...@gmail.com <tegunov@gmail.com> wrote:

> Hi Tsanwen,
>
> it depends on the type of samples you intend to refine. More GPU memory
> will be beneficial if you have many different classes that you want to
> refine simultaneously (the 3D references are held in GPU memory). More CPU
> memory (divided by the number of GPUs) will help if you have a lot of
> particles per field of view at relatively high resolution (e.g.
> EMPIAR-10164 <https://www.ebi.ac.uk/pdbe/emdb/empiar/entry/10164/> with
> its 2000+ high-res particles per tomogram).
>
> The machine I've used for the vast majority of my refinements has 4x 2080
> Ti 11 GB and 128 GB RAM. I had to disable 1 or 2 GPUs in a few cases
> (EMPIAR-10164 was one of them), but this configuration has been sufficient
> for everything else.
>
> Cheers,
> Dimitry
>
> On Monday, October 12, 2020 at 10:00:36 PM UTC+2 tsa...@walterlab.ucsf.edu
> wrote:
>
>> Hi Dimitry,
>>
>> We are trying to build a Warp/M machine for our own tomography/single
>> particle data analysis. (i.e. not for facility so we don't need to process
>> data from the camera on-the-fly)
>>
>> Wondering if the following configuration setup would be enough for
>> running Warp/M.
>> 2x NVIDIA® GeForce RTX™ 2080 Ti 11GB
>> 128GB DDR4 RAM 2933MHz
>> Intel® Core™ i9 10900KF (10-Core, 20MB Cache, 3.7GHz to 5.3GHz)
>>
>> My understanding is that it is better to have GPU with >11GB memory. How
>> about the CPU and memory? are there any requirements for those two?
>>
>> Thank you for your help!
>> Tsanwen
>>
> --
